Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Fields all PR pitches for the program, which covers news and culture from a Latino perspective. Stories must have strong national appeal to the Latino community, but beyond that, "it's pretty broad based," Avila says. Is especially interested in hearing about experts on issues such as immigration and bilingual education. Don't pitch local events, executive promotions or corporate campaigns that target Latinos. "If a company increases the budget for Spanish-language advertising, we're not interested," he says. And "if it's not Latino-related, you're wasting my time." Prefers 10-days' notice for time-sensitive stories. Limited follow-ups are OK, but don't be pushy.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Johnson covers breaking company and financial news, with an emphasis on stories affecting the markets, i.e., public companies or very large private companies. Don't pitch new products, marketing strategies or career workplace issues. Johnson seeks sources who can help him interpret market news for the masses and make predictions of what's to come. "We try to stay one step ahead of the news," Johnson says. For KRLD, he files two- to three-minute segments that run at 5:55, 6:55, 7:25, 7:55, 8:25 and 8:55 a.m. He also does market wraps at 3:25 and 4:25 p.m. Johnson dislikes phone pitches. Keep him posted about your company through the PR newswires. Watch the headlines to know when you can offer your resident expert to explain the significance of market news.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Fields all PR pitches for the program, which covers news and culture from a Latino perspective. Stories must have strong national appeal to the Latino community, but beyond that, "it's pretty broad based," Avila says. Is especially interested in hearing about experts on issues such as immigration and bilingual education. Don't pitch local events, executive promotions or corporate campaigns that target Latinos. "If a company increases the budget for Spanish-language advertising, we're not interested," he says. And "if it's not Latino-related, you're wasting my time." Prefers 10-days' notice for time-sensitive stories. Limited follow-ups are OK, but don't be pushy.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Johnson covers breaking company and financial news, with an emphasis on stories affecting the markets, i.e., public companies or very large private companies. Don't pitch new products, marketing strategies or career workplace issues. Johnson seeks sources who can help him interpret market news for the masses and make predictions of what's to come. "We try to stay one step ahead of the news," Johnson says. For KRLD, he files two- to three-minute segments that run at 5:55, 6:55, 7:25, 7:55, 8:25 and 8:55 a.m. He also does market wraps at 3:25 and 4:25 p.m. Johnson dislikes phone pitches. Keep him posted about your company through the PR newswires. Watch the headlines to know when you can offer your resident expert to explain the significance of market news.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Arnold covers among other things, the housing market and the sub-prime crisis. "Most of us are general audience-we don't want to hear about a computer chip that's ten percent faster," he says. "If your friends wouldn't be interested in your idea shared over the span of a few seconds, then radio listeners won't be either." He adds, "the idea has to be interesting to a sizeable chunk of the populace. A pitch about how hedge funds work won't grab us. But one about how hedge funds killed Bear Stearns might because it's about a huge bank taking, the search for blame and how that impacts everyday people's pocketbooks." Before pitching, conduct online research to figure out whom to contact. "If you can't answer questions toe-to-toe with us about the details your pitching, you will loss credibility," he says. "We'll find a way to get the information we need without you in the loop. Make sure you get briefed by the client or people in your company with the specifics before calling us." Make it through his tough filter by "not dong the salesman thing," he says. "By really being interested, knowledge about and, yes it's possible, even passionate about the pitch." In addition, be selective and "only call when it's really, really good," he says. "They're selective about what they call about and do so only when it's a perfect fit. They know we turn them off if they're always on machine-gun mode."

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Johnson covers breaking company and financial news, with an emphasis on stories affecting the markets, i.e., public companies or very large private companies. Don't pitch new products, marketing strategies or career workplace issues. Johnson seeks sources who can help him interpret market news for the masses and make predictions of what's to come. "We try to stay one step ahead of the news," Johnson says. For KRLD, he files two- to three-minute segments that run at 5:55, 6:55, 7:25, 7:55, 8:25 and 8:55 a.m. He also does market wraps at 3:25 and 4:25 p.m. Johnson dislikes phone pitches. Keep him posted about your company through the PR newswires. Watch the headlines to know when you can offer your resident expert to explain the significance of market news.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Birnbach's weekly talk show covers business, politics and travel from 4 to 5 p.m. every Sunday in the greater Dallas area. The best way to pitch the program is to get to know the host and then talk over your ideas with him directly. Unlike many journalists who thrive on e-mail, Birnbach says that if you pitch via e-mail, he probably won't respond. "Just call me and I'll give an answer right away," he says. A recent show featured ideas on starting a small company and his guests focused on the emotional, political and lifestyle impacts of doing it. He also recently homed in on European travel and spoke to guests who could provide local insight. On the political angle, he's perennially interested in Texas politics, especially immigration. When pitching guests, keep in mind that he's looking for "articulate, passionate people who know what they're talking about." He also expects to give regular coverage to issues in the Middle East.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Weeks produces "Art & Seek," which covers all aspects of art and culture in the North Texas area. Coverage includes film festivals, blues clubs, local authors, live theater, major museums exhibitions and gallery openings, arts funding and arts education issues. "We cover it in radio reports and reviews, online stories and searchable calendar listings," he says. "We also contribute an arts interview segment to the station's `Think' television show." Keep in mind: "The best thing PR people can do is explain who this is, what this is, why it's connected to North Texas and to our interests," he says. "Do this as succinctly and clearly as possible." He's not interested in the tried-and-true PR stories, the cute kids in the show or the artist returning to his high school. "I like stories that let us explain to our listeners something `backstage,' `insider' or `predictive' about the art itself or the art organization," he says. A PR pitch from the Dallas Symphony about their concertmaster's 400 year-old violin resulted in a story. Contact by email.

He's been pitched education stories about how computers help educate low-income kids in public schools and how education software helps deaf and hearing impaired kids. Contact by email.
